Thank you, @Ichimonji10 for providing detailed concerns regarding the suggestion. My desire to tackle this for just the pulp_ansible repo was purely due to wanting to come to consensus quickly on an incremental problem rather than trying to come up with a solution for a more complicated problem. It would also reduce the risk of making a change and also make a change with the most risk tolerant part (pulp_ansible has zero tests now and we aren't asking QE to support/own this now.) We could make the change for a plugin and we could learn from the experience rather than trying to come up with a solution for all plugins and take a longer time to learn what doesn't work and then make a change across all plugins. I see several suggestions here that also accomplish that.

I am fully supportive of us making progress on encouraging pulp-smash contributions on all plugins and coming up with incremental changes that are consistent across plugins (to the degree that they are consistent.) Maybe all productized plugins vs non-productized plugins? I advocate for as simple as possible and only making something more complicated if necessary.

I felt the demo of a new plugin with a call for integration test cases contributions may be helpful since users of that may want to ensure the code continues to function as desired. I would put the pulp_ansible plugin in the non-productized catagory. I ask that QE not devote staffing to writing tests for the pulp_ansible plugin now. I recognize that if that ask changes, it would be nice if they were starting from somewhere above zero test cases and recognize that other stakeholders may have a selfish need to prioritize & possibly do that work. If that non-productized --> productized status changes, happy to revisit policy/planning at that time.

To answer some concern about unit vs. functional testing - I do understand these to be different and distinct. I did not intend to lump these together other in definition or value - just that it would be nice for a contribution to include code+unit test+functional test together. The value I see here is that we still have **an option** of them being done separately but allow them to be contributed together. Now we **have** to submit them in 2 different pull requests. If they are merged in 1 pull request we eliminate a scenario where code is in the main branch that fails a pulp-smash test. I could see future policies where bug fixes for pulp-smash issues that are known to fail could be required to re-enable the test - all without QE effort (although I would not have an issue if QE wants to review those changes).

What is happening here is that `graphqln_sqlalchemy.converter.convert_sqlalchemy_type.register` creates a GraphQL Enum using the enum instances using `items = type.enum_class.__members__.items()`. But in `graphql.type.definition.GraphQLEnumType.serialize` this happens:

```python
    def serialize(self, value):
        # type: (Union[str, PyEnum]) -> Optional[str]
        if isinstance(value, PyEnum):
            # We handle PyEnum values
            value = value.value
        if isinstance(value, collections.Hashable):
            enum_value = self._value_lookup.get(value)
            if enum_value:
                return enum_value.name

        return None
```

at that point the enum.Enum instance is converted to its value, which is then looked up. But the lookup table has the enum instances, causing the lookup to fail and an error to be returned.

On Thu, Feb 15, 2018 at 6:48 PM Nick Felt <notifications@github.com> wrote:

> While I agree with your comment in #900
> <https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/issues/900> that it would be
> good to have a way to explicitly provide the frequencies (or probabilities)
> to the histogram/distribution dashboards, I'm not sure this specific
> approach is ideal because it seems like it conflates several different uses
> of histograms into the same summary method.
>
> The issue I'm seeing is that adding edges to the existing method might
> suggest to users that they can explicitly list the edges of the buckets
> into which the values in data will be automatically binned (rather than
> the bucket edges being automatically determined based on bucket_count and
> the max and min of the data). However, in this PR's implementation, edges
> is incompatible with the current auto-binning behavior because its presence
> causes data to be interpreted as the set of frequencies for each bucket
> instead of values to be binned.
>
> We could largely resolve that by moving this functionality to a new
> summary method with a different name, but I still have a couple concerns
> about specifying edges:
>
>    - explicit edges isn't really well-supported by the current Histograms
>    dashboard, which essentially re-bins everything (as far as I understand)
>    across a linear set of x-values that are spaced across the min and max
>    values of that histogram tag across all steps (not from any single
>    histogram). The result is that the original histogram bin edges in general
>    don't make it forward to the final dashboard display, at least not if the
>    min/max values change over time. I'm curious to know whether you get a
>    display that you actually find useful for your example in #900
>    <https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/issues/900> where you only
>    have 4 buckets, since I believe as implemented the histogram dashboard
>    would re-distribute your frequencies across a whole bunch of smaller tick
>    marks.
>    - explicit edges opens up the possibility of non-linear edges, i.e.
>    buckets of different widths. In that case it's misleading to plot the
>    frequency as the height of the bucket; instead you want to plot the
>    frequency density, i.e. divide out by the width of the bucket. The
>    frontend's re-distribution of frequencies might sort of accidentally
>    accommodate this but it also might not (I haven't tried it) and either way
>    it's not really doing it accurately, so to support non-linear edges we
>    would likely have to rethink some of that logic as well.
>
> We could sidestep those concerns by avoiding explicit edges and instead
> limiting the user to linearly spaced buckets (e.g. letting you specify min
> and max values which then autogenerate bucket_count linearly interpolated
> buckets), but I think we'd want to confirm that this is useful enough and
> weigh the trade off between doing that vs trying to add explicit edges
> support.
>
> Lastly, I'm curious whether in #900
> <https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/issues/900> when you referred
> to categorical probabilities whether your categories have an inherent
> ordering or x-axis dimension to them, like restaurant star ratings (or put
> another way, do fractional values of your x-axis have any meaning?). If
> that isn't the case, strictly speaking you want to plot a bar chart instead
> of a histogram (where ideally the x-axis is labeled by category rather than
> numbered), so the histogram plugin is a sub-optimal workaround for that
> kind of visualization anyway.
>

Ok, so here's what was going on.

In ACMEv1, if you want to unregister, you better have a `jwk` in your header. In ACMEv2, you can have a `kid` in your payload instead, or use `jwk` if you want.

Here's the thing. `jwk` and `kid` are mutually exclusive, so if one is set, we don't include the other. We only set `kid` if a) we're using acmev2 as determined by the `--server` URL, and b) we have an account loaded into `ClientNetwork.account`.

Now. If you have an ACMEv1 account but an ACMEv2 `--server`, we were just posting it to an ACMEv1 url. But we think that we're using ACMEv2, so we set `kid`, there's no `jwk`, and the ACMEv1 server is unhappy.

Slight aside for posterity:
Getting an account using `onlyReturnExisting` was failing because the new-account endpoint on ACMEv2 doesn't accept `kid` at all, but `ClientNetwork.account` was set because there is a valid account hanging around our system so we just stuck that value in there, in an only-talking-about-one-loaded-account-this-run paradigm, which is actually mostly fine.

Anyway, it's actually perfectly fine to go ahead and continue making updates to the ACMEv1 account at the ACMEv1 URL, even if we think we're using ACMEv2. We just need to make sure that `ClientNetwork.account` isn't set for the `deactivate` operation. It's also perfectly fine to unset `ClientNetwork.account`, get the new url, make sure `ClientNetwork.account` is still unset, then deactivate at that new URL.

To actually fix this bug, we can make a one-line change. Or we could try to do fancier things, as we've discussed above. Not sure I see a pressing reason to do so (especially for this release), but willing to be convinced otherwise.

Certbot is an [ACME](https://github.com/ietf-wg-acme/acme) client and ACME dictates that `.well-known/acme-challenge` must be used as the prefix. Making such a plugin would not work at this time because no ACME servers allow you to choose an alternate prefix.

This is outside of the scope of EFF's ACME client. If you would like to see this changed, feel free to reach out to the IETF working group. Alternatively, configure your webserver so requests to `.well-known/acme-challenge` are handled in a way that is compatible with ACME.

To throw out some random unbaked/off-the-cuff ideas/thoughts:
- Given that there are widely different use cases for `certbot-auto`, maybe a one-size-fits all approach isn't the best. Perhaps users could somehow customize the `certbot-auto` they download. (E.g., through a download-time selection process, like [jQuery](https://jqueryui.com/download/) has.)
- Instead of guessing about which plugins are worth packing in `certbot-auto`, perhaps we could make data-driven decisions. We could package each so that it can be downloaded and used (somehow) and keep track of downloads or active lineages or some similar metric and add plugins to `certbot-auto` based on some objective criteria.
- What do we do about the DNS plugins we currently can't ship by default (e.g., because they don't support Python 2.6)? There are three such plugins in the tree now, PRs out for three more, and another 10 or so that would be easy to implement. (The specifics of that might be best discussed in #1279, but I mention it here in part because having a good installation process may influence our decision on what to include by default.)
- What happens to people who using `certbot-auto` on a machine with very limited disk space when we double (or more) the disk space required? (It seems like `certbot-auto` could fill someone's drive, which would be... bad.)
- Could there be a `certbot-auto` with the set of plugins that it includes today and `certbot-auto-extras` (or whatever) with stricter requirements (e.g., Python 2.7+, lots of disk space) that includes _everything_ (these plugins, the Lexicon plugins, the in-progress GoDaddy [#4748] and RFC 2136 [#4701] plugins, additional installer plugins [#4671, #4672, #4673], etc.)?

One thing that would be helpful is to include "quick start" instructions for getting a simple Ray application running from a brand new Ubuntu machine. To try write an application using Ray from Java, I had to do the following.

```
sudo apt install maven default-jdk
```

Then I created a project with `mvn archetype:generate -DgroupId=com.mycompany.app -DartifactId=my-app -DarchetypeArtifactId=maven-archetype-quickstart -DinteractiveMode=false`

Then I had to add the following to the `pom.xml`.

```
    <dependency>
      <groupId>org.ray</groupId>
      <artifactId>ray-api</artifactId>
      <version>1.0</version>
    </dependency>

    <dependency>
      <groupId>org.ray</groupId>
      <artifactId>ray-runtime-common</artifactId>
      <version>1.0</version>
    </dependency>

    <dependency>
      <groupId>org.ray</groupId>
      <artifactId>ray-runtime-native</artifactId>
      <version>1.0</version>
    </dependency>

    <dependency>
      <groupId>org.ray</groupId>
      <artifactId>ray-runtime-dev</artifactId>
      <version>1.0</version>
    </dependency>

    <dependency>
      <groupId>org.ray</groupId>
      <artifactId>ray-common</artifactId>
      <version>1.0</version>
    </dependency>

    <dependency>
      <groupId>org.ray</groupId>
      <artifactId>ray-hook</artifactId>
      <version>1.0</version>
    </dependency>
```

and also

```
  <properties>
    <maven.compiler.source>1.8</maven.compiler.source>
    <maven.compiler.target>1.8</maven.compiler.target>
  </properties>
```

Then I had to add a bunch of imports like

```java
import org.ray.api.Ray;
import org.ray.api.RayRemote;
import org.ray.api.RayActor;
import org.ray.api.RayList;
import org.ray.api.RayObject;
import org.ray.api.WaitResult;
import org.ray.util.logger.RayLog;
```

to the application.

Then I had to duplicate `ray/java/ray.config.ini` and add `%CONFIG_FILE_DIR%/../../my-app/target/my-app-1.0-SNAPSHOT.jar =` under the `[ray.java.path.classes.package]` header.

Then I compiled with `mvn package`.

Then I had to do `export RAY_CONFIG=/path/to/ray.config.ini`.

Then I compiled with `java -Djava.library.path=/home/ubuntu/ray/build/src/plasma/:/home/ubuntu/ray/build/src/local_scheduler/ -cp target/my-app-1.0-SNAPSHOT.jar:/home/ubuntu/ray/java/test/lib/* com.mycompany.app.App`

I think we can come up with much simpler instructions (e.g., we can provide an example so the user doesn't have to do `mvn archetype:generate` and so the `pom.xml` is already in place.

So, my ideal API would be async API where I could subscribe to both stdout/stderr and logging output from all workers. The messages should not just be strings but also have metadata like which worker it is and so on.

In fact, Python logging does most of that already. So what I believe would be the best is that we would redirect all stdout/stderr (and warnings, too) to logging first. And then just provide a way to consolidate those logging messages (so raw logging messages, not the serialized to console ones) together. I have made [a context manager for this already](https://gitlab.com/datadrivendiscovery/d3m/blob/devel/d3m/utils.py#L559).

So, my plan for our system is to capture all stdout/stderr (and warnings) to logging inside a Ray task, and then store all logging to our MongoDB document for that task. The issue I am struggling with is how to do the same for any stdout/stderr, warnings, and logging which happen around running the task itself. I would like to have a way to do something about that. So one option could be to configure each worker this way myself, intercepting everything and doing it myself. Another, probably better for a general case, would be that Ray would be storing that to Redis: so stuff per worker and stuff per each task. And then have an API to or fetch or subscribe to that.

@jupyterlab/services had a major version bump to 2.0.0. The change there practically had no effect on our code (the one change we made in our shim is actually in code we don't use anymore). So we could make the argument that for our users, nothing changed.

However, If someone downstream had both a dependency on services `^1`, and a dependency on base `^1`, now there is a conflict (because the newest version of base actually depends on services 2). For that reason, I plan to make the following bumps:

`@jupyter-widgets/base` from 1.1.9 to 2.0.0
`@jupyter-widgets/controls` from 1.2.0 to 2.0.0
`@jupyter-widgets/html-manager` from 0.12.0 to 0.13.0
`@jupyter-widgets/html-manager` from 0.34.0 to 0.35.0
`@jupyter-widgets/output` from 1.0.16 to 2.0.0

widgetsnbextension python package from 3.2.0 to 3.3.0 (since this is not a js library people depend on)

ipywidgets python package from 7.2.0 to 7.2.1 for a single change, a bump in the html manager version for the embedding code: https://github.com/jupyter-widgets/ipywidgets/blob/master/ipywidgets/_version.py#L17

Note that the actual widget protocol versions stay *exactly* the same. This now leads to a wider divergence between the widget "module_version" protocol number, and the actual npm package version.

I just experimented with Swagger v2.3.1 which supports asyncio+aiohttp based client library generation, so that we could use a "native" async interface instead of interpolating callbacks to futures.

What I have done to make it working, starting from [the kubernetes-client/gen repository](https://github.com/kubernetes-client/gen):
* Set `SWAGGER_CODEGEN_COMMIT` in `openapi/python.sh` to `"v2.3.1"`
* Add `<library>asyncio</library>` under `openapi/python.xml`'s `<configuration>` element.
* Run the generator script as instructed.
* After generation, restructure the generated client library to match [the structure guide](https://github.com/kubernetes/community/blob/master/contributors/design-proposals/api-machinery/csi-client-structure-proposal.md)
  - Relocate the generated `kubernetes` python package to `kubernetes/client`.
  - Add [python-base](https://github.com/kubernetes-client/python-base) as `kubernetes/base` git submodule.
  - Create symbolic links `kubernetes/stream`, `kubernetes/config`, `kubernetes/watch` like this repo
  - Create `kubernetes/__init__.py` like this repo
  - Replace all absolute imports to use relative imports (e.g., `from kubernetes.api_client.apps_v1beta1_deployment import AppsV1beta1Deployment` in the generated code to `from .apps_v1beta1_deployment import AppsV1beta1Deployment`)
  - Fix up circular import errors related to `V1beta1JSONSchemaProps`
  - NOTE: I think `find` and `sed` commands in the generator's `python.sh` script should do the above changes automatically, but it didn't work out of the box. So I did manually.
* Comment out `ssl_verify` argument in `kubernetes/client/api_client.py` since aiohttp forbids passing both `ssl_context` and `ssl_verify` option (the later is included in the former)

Then I could run the following example:
```python
import asyncio
from kubernetes import client, config

loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()

async def do():
    config.load_kube_config()
    v1 = client.CoreV1Api()
    ret = await v1.list_pod_for_all_namespaces(watch=False)
    for i in ret.items:
        print("%s\t%s\t%s" % (i.status.pod_ip, i.metadata.namespace, i.metadata.name))

loop.run_until_complete(do())
loop.close()
```

So the majority of APIs can be converted to use asyncio + aiohttp without much efforts, thanks to Swagger.
Still, the python-base *streaming wrappers* should be converted manually.

> if another static library, let's say, lib A, depends on onnx. it still has to manually add "-DONNX_NAMESPACE=.."

I think it is not the case. As long as a developer use `add_subdirectory` to add onnx sources into his library, the flag will automatically take effect.

In fact, a perfect c++ && cmake project should let downstreams directly use `find_package(xxx)` in CMakeLists.txt if the project has been installed, it is a feature of modern(namely 3.0+, I think) cmake ([link](https://pabloariasal.github.io/2018/02/19/its-time-to-do-cmake-right/)). For example, if onnx supports this feature, then developers can simply link their lib to onnx lib using the following code

```
find_package(onnx)
add_library(my_lib my_lib.cpp)
target_link_libraries(my_lib onnx)
```

on any PC which onnx is installed, the compile flags will automatically take effects too.

Sadly onnx doesn't support it, but my PR also helps when using `add_subdirectory` to directly build onnx sources rather than link the installed onnx lib.

I don't have generalized code yet that I can share, but I can chime in with some basic take aways I've had doing something similar. I've found that with a number of the Async methods, it helps to have a few threads dedicated to running the current policy, but then have a couple other threads that are feeding data from other policies completely unrelated to the state of the neural networks, which could be data from human played games, or perhaps data from the best practices policy. This all gets collected and fed to the buffer, which in turn trains the critic (and updates the actors). I don't think you necessarily just want to preload the buffer with human played games' results though. My gut says it's probably better to mix that data in with what it learns from exploration in the beginning and help nudge it along. Mix those results in, perhaps with a decreasing percentage of samples overtime, kind of like exploration.
